questions.question: What does a fidelity value of 0.95 between actual and expected statevectors indicate?

options.text: The states have 95% overlap, indicating high similarity with small errors

questions.explanation: Fidelity measures the overlap between quantum states as |<actual|expected>|^2. A value of 0.95 means the states have high similarity (95% overlap), but there are small differences that may need debugging.

questions.question: When debugging, you find that amplitudes have correct magnitudes but wrong phases. What type of gates should you check?

options.text: Z, S, T, or Rz rotation gates

questions.explanation: Phase gates (Z, S, T) and Z-axis rotations (Rz) modify the phase of quantum states without changing amplitude magnitudes. If magnitudes are correct but phases are wrong, these are the gates to investigate.

questions.question: What does it mean if a single qubit's reduced density matrix has purity less than 1?

options.text: The qubit is in a superposition state
The qubit is entangled with other qubits in the system

questions.explanation: When a qubit is entangled with other qubits, tracing out those other qubits produces a mixed state with purity < 1. A pure, unentangled qubit would have purity = 1. This is a key indicator of entanglement.

questions.question: Before computing a statevector from a circuit for debugging, what operation must you perform?

options.text: Remove all measurements from the circuit

questions.explanation: Statevector simulation computes the quantum state before any measurement. Measurements collapse the state and cannot be included in statevector computation. Use remove_final_measurements() before simulation.

questions.question: You expect a Bell state |00⟩ + |11⟩ but get |00⟩ + |10⟩. What is the likely error?

options.text: CNOT applied with wrong control/target qubits

questions.explanation: The state |00⟩ + |10⟩ means qubit 0 flipped but qubit 1 didn't correlate. This suggests the CNOT has wrong control/target - the control was on qubit 1 (not flipping) instead of qubit 0 which is in superposition.

questions.question: What does a 'uniform superposition' mean in statevector debugging?

options.text: All basis states have the same phase
All basis states have equal probability amplitudes
Only computational basis states have non-zero amplitude

questions.explanation: A uniform superposition means all 2^n basis states have equal probability (equal amplitude magnitudes). For n qubits, each state has amplitude 1/sqrt(2^n). This is created by applying H gates to all qubits starting from |0...0⟩.

questions.question: Global phase differences between two statevectors affect measurement outcomes and should always be debugged.
questions.explanation: False. Global phase (an overall phase factor multiplying the entire state) has no physical effect - it cannot be detected by any measurement. Only relative phases between basis states matter. States differing only by global phase are physically equivalent.

questions.question: When tracing state evolution, you see the state suddenly become the |0...0⟩ state in the middle of the circuit. What likely happened?

options.text: Either A or C - operations that reset qubits to |0⟩

questions.explanation: Both measurements (which collapse to computational basis) and explicit reset operations can cause the state to return to |0...0⟩. In debugging, finding unexpected |0⟩ states indicates measurement or reset operations in the circuit.
